docs.title: Getting Started with Guppy
docs.text:
Guppy is a Go client and CLI for the Storacha's Forge network, built for enterprise-scale, resumable uploads with parallel processing.

Unlike simple upload tools, Guppy is designed for large datasets that may take hours or days to upload. It tracks progress locally, so interrupted uploads resume exactly where they left off. Data is prepared and uploaded in parallel, which maximizes throughput and allows the data to start uploading early, before the full scan is complete.

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
go install github.com/storacha/guppy@latest

Verify the installation:

guppy version

docs.title: Step 1: Log In
docs.text:
Guppy automatically generates an agent identity on first use. To authorize that identity with your Storacha account:

guppy login you@example.com

Storacha will send a confirmation email. Click the link in the email to authorize Guppy to act on your behalf.

Your identity, proofs, and other state are stored in ~/.storacha/guppy/ by default. You can set a different directory with --data-dir.

docs.title: Step 2: Create a Space
docs.text:
A space is a container for your data on the Storacha network. Each space has a unique did:key: identifier and can also have a human-readable name.

guppy space generate --name my-data

This creates a new space, provisions it to your account for billing, and grants your account full access. Your space is identified by a DID, which looks like did:key:…. Any command which refers to space can take either the DID or the name you gave it. You can list the spaces you have access to at any time:

guppy space list

docs.title: Step 3: Add a Source
docs.text:
A source is a local filesystem path associated with a space. Guppy scans sources to discover files to upload.

guppy upload source add my-data /path/to/dataset

You can add multiple sources to a space. Each one will become a separate upload in the space, each with its own root CID. List a space's sources with:

guppy upload source list my-data

The association of sources to spaces is purely local. Adding a source to a space doesn't affect the space itself on the network, and doesn't affect other instances of guppy on other machines pointing to the same space.

docs.title: Step 4: Upload
docs.text:
Run upload for your space:

guppy upload my-data
# Or, for easier visuals for humans
guppy upload my-data --ui

Guppy processes the upload in stages:

  1. Scan — Walks the source paths to discover files
  2. DAG — Breaks files into a DAG of UnixFS nodes
  3. Shard & Index — Packs nodes into shards for storage, and creates indexes into the shards
  4. Upload — Uploads shards and indexes to the Storacha network

These stages form a pipeline, so as soon as a file is discovered, it will be broken into UnixFS nodes, and then those nodes will be packed into shards. As shards are filled, they will be indexed and then uploaded. Shard uploads happen in parallel.

All progress is tracked in a local SQLite database. If the process is interrupted at any stage, just run the upload command on the space again to resume.

docs.title: Resuming uploads
docs.text:
Running upload on a space with a source which has been partially uploaded resumes the upload process from where it left off.

By default, when resuming, upload will scan the entire source again, in case any files have changed between runs. This happens in parallel with resuming any other in-progress work, so other work does not have to wait for the scan to complete before it can begin, but the entire upload process can't complete until the scan has completed and guppy is satisfied that the current state of the source is what it has uploaded to the network.

If you know the source data hasn't changed, you can use --assume-unchanged-sources. This will skip scanning files and directories which have been scanned before, so interrupted filesystem scans will resume rather than restart, and completed filesystem scans will not be repeated. For large sources, especially with many individual files, this is much faster than a full rescan.

guppy upload my-data --assume-unchanged-sources

docs.title: Step 5: Retrieve
docs.text:
Retrieve content from the network:

guppy retrieve my-data <cid> ./output

The content path supports several formats:

  • <cid> — Retrieve a file/directory with the given root CID
  • <cid>/path/to/file — Retrieve a file/directory by path below a given root CID
  • /ipfs/<cid> or ipfs://<cid> — IPFS-style paths

docs.title: Running a Gateway
docs.text:
To provide HTTP access, you can run a local IPFS Gateway:

guppy gateway serve --port 3000

This starts an IPFS Gateway that serves content from your spaces. Access it at http://localhost:3000/ipfs/<cid>. Provide a space name or DID to limit the gateway to that space. The gateway is suitable for using locally or exposing to the public internet. When making it publicly available, we recommend placing it behind a TLS endpoint and serving HTTPS.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Guppy can be configured through CLI flags, environment variables (prefixed with GUPPY_), and a TOML config file. See Configuration for the full reference, including all available config keys, network presets, and the complete config file syntax.

docs.title: What's Next
docs.text:
• Configuration — Config file reference, environment variables, and network presets
  • CLI Reference — Complete documentation for all commands and flags

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Guppy can be configured through three layers, which override each other in order of precedence (highest to lowest):

  1. CLI flags — Override everything (e.g., --port 8080)
  2. Environment variables — Prefixed with GUPPY_ (e.g., GUPPY_GATEWAY_PORT=8080)
  3. Config file — TOML format

docs.title: Config File
docs.text:
Guppy looks for a TOML config file in these locations, reading only the first file found:

  1. Path specified with --config
  2. ~/.config/guppy/config.toml
  3. ./config.toml (current directory)

docs.title: Complete Reference
docs.text:
# Repository configuration — where Guppy stores its data.
[repo]
  # Directory for the SQLite database and agent store.
  data_dir = "~/.storacha/guppy"

  # PostgreSQL connection URL. If set, Guppy uses PostgreSQL instead of SQLite.
  # Format: postgres://user:password@host:port/dbname
  # database_url = "postgres://user:password@localhost:5432/guppy"

# Network configuration — which Storacha network to use.
[network]
  # Preset network name. Sets all the service URLs and DIDs below.
  # Available presets: forge, hot, warm-staging
  name = "forge"

  # Override individual network endpoints (optional).
  # These take precedence over the preset values.
  # upload_id = "did:web:up.forge.storacha.network"
  # upload_url = "https://up.forge.storacha.network"
  # receipts_url = "https://up.forge.storacha.network/receipt/"
  # indexer_id = "did:web:indexer.forge.storacha.network"
  # indexer_url = "https://indexer.forge.storacha.network"
  # authorized_retrievals = true

# Gateway configuration — settings for `guppy gateway serve`.
[gateway]
  # Port to listen on.
  port = 3000

  # Number of blocks to cache in memory. Blocks are typically <1MB due to IPFS
  # chunking, so 1000 blocks ≈ up to 1GB of memory.
  block_cache_capacity = 1000

  # Enable trusted gateway mode, allowing deserialized responses.
  # See: https://docs.ipfs.tech/reference/http/gateway/#trusted-vs-trustless
  trusted = true

  # Logging level for the gateway server.
  # Options: debug, info, warn, error
  log_level = "warn"

  # External HTTPS URL where the gateway is publicly accessible.
  # Required for Kubo retrieval integration. Enables /routing/v1/ endpoints.
  # advertise_url = "https://gateway.example.com"

  # Subdomain gateway configuration.
  # When enabled, content is served at https://<cid>.ipfs.<host>/ and
  # path-style requests (/ipfs/<cid>) are redirected to the subdomain form.
  # See: https://specs.ipfs.tech/http-gateways/subdomain-gateway/
  [gateway.subdomain]
    enabled = false
    # Public host(s) for the gateway. Required if subdomain mode is enabled.
    # hosts = ["gateway.example.com"]

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text: Environment variables use the GUPPY_ prefix. Dots in config keys become underscores.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: Variable Config Key GUPPY_REPO_DATA_DIR repo.data_dir GUPPY_NETWORK_NAME network.name GUPPY_GATEWAY_PORT gateway.port (and so on.)

docs.title: Legacy Variables
docs.text:
These STORACHA_* environment variables are still supported for backward compatibility. They are only used if the corresponding GUPPY_* variable is not set.

Variable Equivalent STORACHA_NETWORK GUPPY_NETWORK_NAME STORACHA_SERVICE_DID GUPPY_NETWORK_UPLOAD_ID STORACHA_SERVICE_URL GUPPY_NETWORK_UPLOAD_URL STORACHA_RECEIPTS_URL GUPPY_NETWORK_RECEIPTS_URL STORACHA_INDEXING_SERVICE_DID GUPPY_NETWORK_INDEXER_ID STORACHA_INDEXING_SERVICE_URL GUPPY_NETWORK_INDEXER_URL STORACHA_AUTHORIZED_RETRIEVALS GUPPY_NETWORK_AUTHORIZED_RETRIEVALS

docs.title: Data Directory
docs.text:
The data directory (~/.storacha/guppy/ by default) contains:

  • SQLite database — Tracks upload progress (scans, DAGs, shards, blobs)
  • Agent store — Your agent identity and UCAN delegations/proofs

Override with --data-dir or GUPPY_REPO_DATA_DIR.

docs.title: Retriable upload issues
docs.text: Sometimes guppy will notice something inconsistent during the upload, like data that changed during the upload process. When this happens, it will print an error and explain that you can simply resume the upload to resolve the problem. To automatically retry in those cases, pass --retry to guppy upload.

docs.title: Bad database states
docs.text: If the database does get into an incorrect state for some reason, or if you're ever concerned it has, you can check the local upload records to make sure they're consistent with guppy upload check. This will run through several integrity checks to ensure the database is in a good state and the upload can continue. Pass --repair to solve any found problems, if possible.

docs.title: DAG issues in the uploaded data
docs.text:
Once uploaded, your root CID should be the root of a UnixFS DAG representing the source data, and all of the nodes of that DAG should be available in your space. You can verify your data is complete and correctly stored using guppy verify:

guppy verify <root-cid>

Note that this requires reading the data from your space, which will incur egress charges. <root-cid> is the CID of the root of the DAG to verify, which can be the root of the entire upload or of some file or directory within the upload, which will verify the contents of that file or directory.

You can list your space's uploads to see their root CIDs:

guppy ls my-data

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text: Guppy provides a command-line interface for uploading, retrieving, and managing data on the Storacha network.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy [command] [flags]

docs.title: Global Flags
docs.text: Flag Description Default --data-dir <path> Directory for config and data store ~/.storacha/guppy --database-url <url> PostgreSQL connection URL (uses SQLite if not set) --config <path> Path to config file --network, -n <name> Network preset name (forge, hot, warm-staging) --ui Use the Guppy TUI --upload-service-did <did> Override network upload service DID --upload-service-url <url> Override network upload service URL --receipts-url <url> Override receipts service URL --indexer-did <did> Override indexing service DID --indexer-url <url> Override indexing service URL

docs.title: login
docs.text: Authenticate with a Storacha account.

docs.title: whoami
docs.text: Print information about the local agent.

docs.title: version
docs.text: Print the version of Guppy.

docs.title: reset
docs.text: Reset agent proofs (log out from all accounts).

docs.title: list
docs.text: List uploads in a space.

docs.title: retrieve
docs.text: Retrieve a file or directory by its CID.

docs.title: verify
docs.text: Verify a DAG's integrity and correctness.

docs.title: account
docs.text: Manage logged-in accounts.

docs.title: space
docs.text: Create and manage spaces.

docs.title: upload
docs.text: Upload data to the Storacha network.

docs.title: delegation
docs.text: Delegate capabilities to other agents.

docs.title: proof
docs.text: Manage proofs delegated to this agent.

docs.title: gateway
docs.text: Run an IPFS Gateway on the Storacha network.

docs.title: blob
docs.text: List blobs in a space.

docs.title: unixfs
docs.text: Inspect UnixFS content in a space.

docs.title: list
docs.text:
List uploads in a space.

Displays all uploads as CIDs (Content Identifiers). Optionally shows the shard CIDs under each upload root.

This is a network operation. Your agent must be authorized to upload/list before running this command. This typically delegated to any account granted access to a space.

Aliases: ls, list

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy ls <space> [flags]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description space Space DID or name

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Description --proof <path> Path to a CAR archive containing additional UCAN proofs --shards Display shard CIDs under each upload root

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
List uploads by space name:

guppy ls my-space

List uploads by space DID:

guppy ls did:key:z6MksCX5PdUgHv83cmDE2DfCrR1WHG9MmZPRKSvTi8Ca297V

List uploads with shard details:

guppy ls my-space --shards

docs.title: login
docs.text:
Authenticate with a Storacha account.

This initiates an authorization email flow. Guppy sends a request to the Storacha network, which emails you a confirmation link. Clicking the link authorizes your local Guppy agent to act on behalf of your account.

You can log into multiple accounts by running this command more than once with different email addresses. Re-running with an existing account will refresh your delegations, which is useful if you've been granted access to new spaces.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy login <email-address>

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description email-address Your Storacha account email address

docs.title: Example
docs.text: guppy login racha@storacha.network

docs.title: reset
docs.text:
Reset agent proofs.

Removes all proofs and delegations from the local store, effectively logging out from all accounts. Your agent DID is preserved. Only the authorization proofs are removed.

After resetting, you'll need to run guppy login again to re-authorize.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy reset

docs.title: retrieve
docs.text:
Retrieve a file or directory by its CID from the Storacha network.

Downloads content from a space and writes it to the specified output path.

Your agent must be authorized to space/content/retrieve before running this command. This typically delegated to any account granted access to a space.

Aliases: retrieve, get

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy retrieve <space> <content-path> <output-path>

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text:
Argument Description space Space DID or name content-path CID or content path to retrieve output-path Local path to write the retrieved content

The content-path supports several formats:

  • /ipfs/<cid>[/<subpath>]
  • ipfs://<cid>[/<subpath>]
  • <cid>[/<subpath>]

Using a subpath retrieves a specific file or directory beneath the root CID.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Retrieve data at a root CID:

guppy retrieve myspace bafybeibhybbpoqakv7pfj5nlrpmldkgiuksmbi3t2cnhxqxnqvbzkhyzjy ./output

Retrieve a file at a subpath:

guppy retrieve myspace bafybeibhybbpoqakv7pfj5nlrpmldkgiuksmbi3t2cnhxqxnqvbzkhyzjy/photos/cat.jpg ./cat.jpg

docs.title: verify
docs.text:
Verify the integrity and correctness of a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph).

Launches a TUI that shows verification progress including block counts, shards verified, and origin nodes checked. Use this to confirm that uploaded data is complete and correctly stored on the network.

This is a network operation. Your agent must be authorized to space/content/retrieve from a space containing the data before running this command. This typically delegated to any account granted access to a space.

Note that this command retrieves data to verify it, and will incur egress charges.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy verify <root-cid>

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description root-cid The root CID of the DAG to verify

docs.title: Example
docs.text: guppy verify bafybeibhybbpoqakv7pfj5nlrpmldkgiuksmbi3t2cnhxqxnqvbzkhyzjy

docs.title: version
docs.text:
Print the version of Guppy.

Displays the version number, git commit, build date, and builder information.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy version

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
guppy version

guppy version 0.1.0
  commit: abc1234
  built:  2025-01-15T10:30:00Z
  by:     goreleaser

docs.title: whoami
docs.text:
Print information about the local agent.

Displays the agent's DID (Decentralized Identifier). Guppy automatically generates an agent identity on first use and stores it in the data directory.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy whoami

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
guppy whoami

did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK

docs.title: account
docs.text: Manage logged-in Storacha accounts.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy account [command]

docs.title: list
docs.text: List all logged-in accounts.

docs.title: account list
docs.text:
List all Storacha accounts currently logged in.

Aliases: list, ls

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy account list [flags]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Description --json Output in JSON format

docs.title: Example
docs.text: guppy account list

docs.title: blob
docs.text: Inspect blobs stored in a space.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy blob [command]

docs.title: list
docs.text: List blobs in a space.

docs.title: blob list
docs.text:
List blobs stored in a space.

Aliases: list, ls

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy blob list <space> [flags]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description space Space DID or name

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Description --proof <path> Path to a CAR archive containing UCAN proofs --long, -l Display detailed blob information --human, -H Use human-readable size format (with --long) --json Output as newline-delimited JSON

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
List blobs:

guppy blob list my-data

List with sizes in human-readable format:

guppy blob list my-data --long --human

Output as JSON:

guppy blob list my-data --json

docs.title: delegation
docs.text: Delegate capabilities for a space to other agents.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy delegation [command]

docs.title: create
docs.text: Create a delegation for a space.

docs.title: delegation create
docs.text:
Delegate capabilities for a space to another agent.

Outputs a CAR-encoded UCAN delegation that grants the specified capabilities to the audience DID. The delegation can be written to a file or piped to another process.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy delegation create <space> <audience-did> [flags]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description space Space DID or name audience-did DID of the agent to delegate capabilities to

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Description --can, -c <ability> Ability to delegate (required, can be specified multiple times) --expiration, -e <timestamp> Unix timestamp for delegation expiration (0 = no expiration) --output, -o <path> Path to write the delegation CAR file (stdout if not set)

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Delegate upload capability to another agent:

guppy delegation create my-data did:key:z6MkhaXgBZDvotDkL5257faiztiGiC2QtKLGpbnnEGta2doK \
  --can space/blob/add \
  --can space/index/add \
  --can filecoin/offer \
  --can upload/add \
  -o delegation.car

The recipient can then add this delegation as a proof:

guppy proof add delegation.car

See guppy proof add for details.

docs.title: gateway
docs.text: Run an IPFS Gateway on the Storacha network.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy gateway [command]

docs.title: serve
docs.text: Start the gateway server.

docs.title: gateway serve
docs.text:
Start an IPFS Gateway that serves content from the Storacha network.

This is a long-running process that operates an IPFS Gateway for your data. By default it serves all spaces you are authorized to access. You can restrict it to specific spaces by passing them as arguments.

The gateway can be configured via command line flags, environment variables (prefix GUPPY_GATEWAY_), or a TOML config file.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy gateway serve [space...] [flags]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description space Optional space DIDs or names to restrict content served (serves all authorized spaces if omitted)

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Description Default --port, -p <port> HTTP port 3000 --block-cache-capacity, -c <n> Number of blocks to cache in memory 1000 --advertise-url <url> External HTTPS URL for delegated routing responses (required for Kubo retrieval) --subdomain, -s Enable subdomain gateway mode (e.g., <cid>.ipfs.<host>) --host <host> Gateway host(s) for subdomain mode (required if subdomain enabled) --trusted, -t Enable trusted gateway mode allowing deserialized responses true --log-level <level> Logging level (debug, info, warn, error)

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Start a gateway on port 8080:

guppy gateway serve --port 8080

Serve a specific space:

guppy gateway serve my-data --port 3000

Start with subdomain mode:

guppy gateway serve --subdomain --host gateway.example.com --port 3000

docs.title: Configuration File
docs.text:
The gateway can also be configured via TOML:

[gateway]
  block_cache_capacity = 1000
  log_level = "warn"
  port = 3000
  trusted = true

[gateway.subdomain]
  enabled = true
  hosts = ["gateway.example.com"]

Place the config file at ~/.config/guppy/config.toml for automatic loading, or specify a path with --config.

docs.title: Stopping the Gateway
docs.text: Use Ctrl+C to gracefully shut down the gateway.

docs.title: proof
docs.text: Manage proofs delegated to this agent.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy proof [command]

docs.title: add
docs.text: Add a proof delegated to this agent.

docs.title: proof add
docs.text:
Add a proof (delegation) to this agent.

Parses a delegation from a data string or file path and adds it to the local proof store. The delegation's audience must match the local agent's DID.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy proof add <data-or-path>

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description data-or-path Delegation data (inline) or path to a CAR file containing the delegation

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Add a delegation from a file:

guppy proof add delegation.car

docs.title: space
docs.text:
Create and manage Storacha spaces.

A space is a container for your uploads on the Storacha network, identified by a did:key: DID. Spaces can also have human-readable names for convenience.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy space [command]

docs.title: generate
docs.text: Generate a new space.

docs.title: list
docs.text: List all known spaces.

docs.title: info
docs.text: Get information about a space.

docs.title: provision
docs.text: Provision a space with a customer account.

docs.title: space generate
docs.text:
Generate a new Storacha space.

Creates a new space, provisions it to your logged-in account (for billing), and grants your account access to the space. The delegations are stored in the network and in your local store.

If you are logged into multiple accounts, use --grant-to and --provision-to to specify which account to use for each.

Spaces can always be referred to in other commands by their full DID. They can also be referred to by their name, if set with --name.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: You must be logged in with guppy login before generating a space.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy space generate [flags]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Description --name <name> Human-readable name for the space --grant-to <did> Account DID to grant access to (optional if only one account logged in) --provision-to <did> Account DID to provision the space to (optional if only one account logged in) --output-key, -k Output the space's private key (sensitive!)

docs.title: Example
docs.text: guppy space generate --name my-data

docs.title: space info
docs.text:
Get information about a space, including its associated providers and provisioning status.

This is a network operation. Your agent must be authorized to space/info before running this command. This typically delegated to any account granted access to a space.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy space info <space> [flags]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description space Space DID or name

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Description --json Output in JSON format

docs.title: Example
docs.text: guppy space info my-data

docs.title: space list
docs.text:
List all Storacha spaces the local agent is aware of, which is all of the spaces it has some kind of access to. Typically, this consists of the spaces which have granted access to an account which the local agent is logged in as.

If your account has been granted access to a space since the local agent logged in, that space won't appear yet. Use guppy login again to fetch the latest spaces you have access to.

Aliases: list, ls

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy space list [flags]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Description --json Output in JSON format

docs.title: Example
docs.text: guppy space list

docs.title: space provision
docs.text:
Provision a space with a customer account for billing purposes.

Associates a space with a customer account so that storage usage is billed to that account. This is typically handled automatically by guppy space generate, but can be done separately if needed.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy space provision <space> <email-address>

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description space Space DID or name email-address Customer account email address

docs.title: Example
docs.text: guppy space provision my-data billing@example.com

docs.title: unixfs
docs.text: Inspect UnixFS content stored on the Storacha network.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy unixfs [command]

docs.title: ls
docs.text: List directory contents.

docs.title: unixfs ls
docs.text: List the contents of a UnixFS directory stored on the Storacha network.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy unixfs ls <space-did> <cid-path> [flags]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description space-did Space DID cid-path CID path in the format <cid>[/<subpath>]

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Description --long, -l Use long listing format with mode, size, date, and name

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
List a directory:

guppy unixfs ls did:key:z6Mkk89bC3JrVqKie71YEcc5M1SMVxuCgNx6zLZ8SYJsxALi bafybeibhybbpoqakv7pfj5nlrpmldkgiuksmbi3t2cnhxqxnqvbzkhyzjy

List with details:

guppy unixfs ls did:key:z6Mkk89bC3JrVqKie71YEcc5M1SMVxuCgNx6zLZ8SYJsxALi bafybeibhybbpoqakv7pfj5nlrpmldkgiuksmbi3t2cnhxqxnqvbzkhyzjy --long

List a subdirectory:

guppy unixfs ls did:key:z6Mkk89bC3JrVqKie71YEcc5M1SMVxuCgNx6zLZ8SYJsxALi bafybeibhybbpoqakv7pfj5nlrpmldkgiuksmbi3t2cnhxqxnqvbzkhyzjy/photos

docs.title: upload
docs.text:
Upload data to a Storacha space.

Aliases: upload, up

Guppy processes the upload in stages:

  1. Scan — Walks the source paths to discover files
  2. DAG — Breaks files into a DAG of UnixFS nodes
  3. Shard & Index — Packs nodes into shards for storage, and creates indexes into the shards
  4. Upload — Uploads shards and indexes to the Storacha network

These stages form a pipeline, so as soon as a file is discovered, it will be broken into UnixFS nodes, and then those nodes will be packed into shards. As shards are filled, they will be indexed and then uploaded. Shard uploads happen in parallel.

All progress is tracked in a local SQLite database. If the process is interrupted at any stage, just run the upload command on the space again to resume.

docs.title: Catching changed sources
docs.text:
By default, when resuming, upload will scan the entire source again, in case any files have changed. The result will be a complete, consistent snapshot of the source. Any work that was in progress in later stages will continue, but the upload will not complete until the re-scan has confirmed that nothing has changed.

If you know the source data hasn't changed, you can use --assume-unchanged-sources. This will skip scanning files and directories which have been scanned before, so interrupted filesystem scans will resume rather than restart, and completed filesystem scans will not be repeated.

guppy upload my-data --assume-unchanged-sources

Guppy will also verify during upload that the data it's sending is what was scanned and hashed originally. If something's become inconsistent, those files will be marked as un-scanned, and resuming the upload will perform a new scan on them. You can also use --retry to automatically retry the upload after hitting such an issue.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy upload <space> [source-path-or-name...] [flags]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description space Space DID or name source-path-or-name Optional specific sources to upload (uploads all sources if omitted)

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Description Default --all Upload all sources even if specific ones are provided as arguments --retry Automatically retry failed uploads --parallelism <n> Number of parallel shard uploads 6 --assume-unchanged-sources Skip filesystem rescan if a completed scan exists (faster resume)

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Upload all sources for a space:

guppy upload my-data

Upload with higher parallelism:

guppy upload my-data --parallelism 12

Resume an interrupted upload without re-scanning:

guppy upload my-data --assume-unchanged-sources

docs.title: source
docs.text: Manage upload sources (local paths associated with a space).

docs.title: check
docs.text: Check upload integrity and completeness.

docs.title: upload check
docs.text:
Check upload integrity and completeness.

Runs a comprehensive validation of the local upload state with six check types:

  1. Upload Scanned - Verifies filesystem and DAG scans completed
  2. File System Integrity - Validates filesystem structure and DAG integrity
  3. Node Integrity - Ensures all nodes have upload records
  4. Node Completeness - Verifies all nodes are packed into shards
  5. Shard Completeness - Verifies all shards are uploaded and indexed
  6. Index Completeness - Verifies all indexes are uploaded

By default, runs in dry-run mode and only reports issues. Use --repair to automatically fix problems when possible.

This is not a network operation. This check operation only looks at your local upload database to check and repair an upload which is not progressing properly.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy upload check [space] [source-path-or-name] [flags]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text:
All arguments are optional. Omit both to check all uploads.

Argument Description space Space DID or name (check all spaces if omitted) source-path-or-name Specific source to check (check all sources in space if omitted)

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Description --repair Automatically fix issues found (default is dry-run)

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Check all uploads:

guppy upload check

Check a specific space:

guppy upload check my-data

Check and repair:

guppy upload check my-data --repair

docs.title: upload source
docs.text:
Manage upload sources for a space.

A source is a local filesystem path associated with a space. Guppy scans sources to discover files to upload.

You can add multiple sources to a space. Each one will become a separate upload in the space, each with its own root CID.

The association of sources to spaces is purely local. Adding a source to a space doesn't affect the space itself on the network, and doesn't affect other instances of guppy on other machines pointing to the same space.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy upload source [command]

docs.title: add
docs.text: Add a source to a space.

docs.title: list
docs.text: List sources added to a space.

docs.title: upload source add
docs.text:
Add a local filesystem path as an upload source for a space.

Associates a path with a space so that guppy upload will include it. You can optionally set a custom name and shard size.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy upload source add <space> <path> [flags]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description space Space DID or name path Local filesystem path to the data

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Description --name <name> Alias name for the source --shard-size <size> Shard size (e.g., 1024, 512B, 100K, 50M, 2G)

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Add a directory as a source:

guppy upload source add my-data /path/to/dataset

Add with a custom name and shard size:

guppy upload source add my-data /path/to/dataset --name "photos" --shard-size 50M

docs.title: upload source list
docs.text:
List all sources associated with a space.

Aliases: list, ls

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: guppy upload source list <space>

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Description space Space DID or name

docs.title: Example
docs.text: guppy upload source list my-data
